items.correct: As a nasal vowel - the n is not fully pronounced.
items.why: Students pronounce nasal vowels like English consonants. French 'on', 'an', 'in', 'un' are nasalised vowels - air resonates through the nose but the n consonant itself isn't articulated.

items.q: What is the correct form of 'parler' (to speak) for 'nous'?
items.why: Students apply the same ending across persons. ER verbs use different endings: je parle, tu parles, il parle, NOUS parlons, vous parlez, ils parlent. Each subject takes its own ending.

items.q: How do you say 'I do not eat' in French?
items.why: Students forget the verb 'sandwich'. Ne and pas wrap AROUND the verb in present tense - ne BEFORE the verb, pas AFTER. In compound tenses, they wrap around the auxiliary.
Correct answer: 5. “15” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Dix-sept. “Onze” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The real figure is 10. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
